episode.title: Cariboo Signals - Wild Spaces & Outdoor Life
episode.description:
Riley and Casey explore technology and society in rural communities. Today's focus: Wild Spaces & Outdoor Life.

NEWS ROUNDUP: We break down [Al Jazeera English] Bolivian farmers clash with police as ex-president vows resistance and [Boing Boing] In 1932, John W. Campbell imagined machines outliving humanity, plus 1 more stories, and explore what these developments mean for communities like ours.

RURAL CONNECTIONS: How can wildlife management frameworks keep pace with changing wild spaces, particularly regarding grizzly population recovery and human encroachment in the Chilcotin? Topics include: Yellowstone camping ban and grizzly population recovery as management case study, Jurisdictional gaps between provincial wildlife management and Tŝilhqot'in Aboriginal title in the Chilcotin, Remote monitoring technology capabilities and cost-benefit analysis, Sustainability challenges of grant-dependent monitoring infrastructure, Pre-trip stewardship communication protocols as governance model.

Hosts: Riley (Tech optimist with an engineering background) and Casey (Community-first skeptic of tech promises).

COMMUNITY SPOTLIGHT: Cariboo Family Enrichment Centre

discussion.central_question: How can wildlife management frameworks keep pace with changing wild spaces, particularly regarding grizzly population recovery and human encroachment in the Chilcotin?
discussion.riley_position: Modern monitoring technology (camera traps, acoustic systems, occupancy models) can enable preventive wildlife management by providing real-time data on animal movement before incidents occur, and the costs of deployment have become accessible to community organizations.
riley_key_evidence: Grizzly populations in Greater Yellowstone Ecosystem recovered significantly over 30 years but now compete with humans for same corridors
Parks Canada uses camera trap arrays and occupancy models in some areas to flag elevated activity before incidents
Trail camera network costs have dropped dramatically, deployable by community groups for thousands of dollars
discussion.casey_position: Technology alone cannot solve systemic governance failures; the real problem is that provincial wildlife management frameworks ignore Tŝilhqot'in Aboriginal title and stewardship authority, and monitoring systems fail without sustained funding and institutional commitment.
casey_key_evidence: Cariboo-Chilcotin management frameworks haven't been updated despite grizzly population pressures from habitat fragmentation and increased backcountry traffic
Recreation site signage reflects province-approved information that contradicts current Tŝilhqot'in land use decisions
Community monitoring systems like iNaturalist concentrate data in most-visited corridors, not highest-risk areas, and grant-funded monitoring stops functioning when funding ends
discussion.resolution: They reached partial agreement that the core goal should be establishing formal governance standing and communication channels for Tŝilhqot'in stewardship information (like Haida's Gwaii Haanas co-management model), with Riley conceding that political agreement is harder than technology implementation and Casey acknowledging such a system is achievable without new platforms.
topics_covered: Yellowstone camping ban and grizzly population recovery as management case study
Jurisdictional gaps between provincial wildlife management and Tŝilhqot'in Aboriginal title in the Chilcotin
Remote monitoring technology capabilities and cost-benefit analysis
Sustainability challenges of grant-dependent monitoring infrastructure
Pre-trip stewardship communication protocols as governance model
